chunk_summary: This section addresses lactation complications such as mastitis, plugged ducts, and nipple thrush, focusing on management strategies, safe medication use, and breastfeeding continuation during treatment.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and management of mastitis, plugged ducts, and nipple thrush in lactating mothers.
Learn safe breastfeeding practices during maternal illness or medication use.
Identify when to initiate antibiotics and assess for complications like abscess formation.
Recognize symptoms of nipple and breast candidiasis and implement effective treatment for both mother and infant.
key_facts: Engorgement and plugged ducts are managed with frequent milk removal, gentle massage, cold packs, and warm compresses before feeding.
Mastitis presents with focal breast pain, erythema, fever, and systemic symptoms; antibiotics targeting Staphylococcus aureus are recommended for severe cases.
MRSA coverage may be necessary based on local epidemiology or treatment failure.
Abscesses require ultrasound-guided drainage, which often allows continued breastfeeding.
Nipple thrush is characterized by burning pain, shiny/flaky areola, and infant oral thrush; both mother and infant require treatment to prevent reinfection.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Continue breastfeeding or expressing from the affected breast unless pain is prohibitive; milk stasis worsens infection and the infant can safely receive the milk. (Source: CDC)
document_key_topics: Management of mastitis and plugged ducts
Treatment of nipple thrush and candidiasis
Safe breastfeeding during maternal illness or medication use
Guidelines for antibiotic use and abscess management
